citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
The licensee did not provide frequent communication to their attending veterinarian to convey timely and accurate
information about ongoing health problems of an older aged adult male Scimitar-horned oryx with poor body condition.
The APHIS official observed that the animal has minimal fat cover throughout its body. His ribs and hip bones are
prominent, and his abdomen is tucked in giving a hollow appearance in the flank area. The facility informed the APHIS
official that they are aware of this animal’s body condition and have notified their veterinarian. The facility provided an
exam record showing that on March 11, 2025, the veterinarian noted that this animal was underweight, giving him a body
condition score of 3/9. The facility informed the APHIS official that at that time they moved the herd of oryx to a separate
pasture for monitoring and separate feedings. The facility informed the APHIS official that they moved them back to the
larger pasture with the facility’s other exotic hoof stock two weeks ago. The same veterinarian did an onsite visit again in
February of this year but did not observe this animal on his walk through of the facility.
The APHIS official called the veterinarian on 5/14/26 and he stated the facility contacted him around the time of the
inspection to let him know the animal was still thin. The facility provided an image to him at this time to show the animal’s
current body condition. The veterinarian stated that based on this image the animal is now at a body condition score of
1.5/9, making him severely underweight. The veterinarian informed the APHIS official that prior to this, the last
communication they received from the facility about this animal’s condition occurred approximately two months ago. The
veterinarian stated that all that was communicated to him at that time was that the animal was "thin." The veterinarian
stated that at that time, he instructed the facility to increase the amount of feed given and to isolate this animal and the
herd in a smaller pasture for monitoring. The veterinarian stated that the severity of the animal’s body condition was not
communicated with him. He stated that had the facility communicated on the amount of weight loss the animal had, he
would have intervened sooner. The attending veterinarian let the APHIS official know that they provided a standard
operating procedure to the facility on 5/14/26 for guidance on the management for the low body condition of the Scimitar-
Horned Oryx.
Frequent, timely, and clear communication on an animal’s health with the attending veterinarian is necessary to guide
adequate treatment. Failure to do so will prolong animal suffering and affect their overall quality of life.
The licensee must have a mechanism of direct and frequent communication so that timely and accurate information on
problems of animal health, behavior, and well-being is conveyed to the attending veterinarian.
Correct by 5/15/26
Corrected on 5/14/26
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative.
n

narrative:
2.40(b)(3)  Direct
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
The licensee did not provide frequent communication to their attending veterinarian to convey timely and accurate 
information about ongoing health problems of an older aged adult male Scimitar-horned oryx with poor body condition. 
The APHIS official observed that the animal has minimal fat cover throughout its body. His ribs and hip bones are 
prominent, and his abdomen is tucked in giving a hollow appearance in the flank area. The facility informed the APHIS 
official that they are aware of this animal’s body condition and have notified their veterinarian. The facility provided an 
exam record showing that on March 11, 2025, the veterinarian noted that this animal was underweight, giving him a body 
condition score of 3/9. The facility informed the APHIS official that at that time they moved the herd of oryx to a separate 
pasture for monitoring and separate feedings. The facility informed the APHIS official that they moved them back to the 
larger pasture with the facility’s other exotic hoof stock two weeks ago. The same veterinarian did an onsite visit again in 
February of this year but did not observe this animal on his walk through of the facility.  
 
The APHIS official called the veterinarian on 5/14/26 and he stated the facility contacted him around the time of the 
inspection to let him know the animal was still thin. The facility provided an image to him at this time to show the animal’s 
current body condition. The veterinarian stated that based on this image the animal is now at a body condition score of 
1.5/9, making him severely underweight. The veterinarian informed the APHIS official that prior to this, the last 
communication they received from the facility about this animal’s condition occurred approximately two months ago. The 
veterinarian stated that all that was communicated to him at that time was that the animal was "thin." The veterinarian 
stated that at that time, he instructed the facility to increase the amount of feed given and to isolate this animal and the 
herd in a smaller pasture for monitoring. The veterinarian stated that the severity of the animal’s body condition was not 
communicated with him. He stated that had the facility communicated on the amount of weight loss the animal had, he 
would have intervened sooner. The attending veterinarian let the APHIS official know that they provided a standard 
operating procedure to the facility on 5/14/26 for guidance on the management for the low body condition of the Scimitar-
Horned Oryx. 
 
Frequent, timely, and clear communication on an animal’s health with the attending veterinarian is necessary to guide 
adequate treatment. Failure to do so will prolong animal suffering and affect their overall quality of life.  
 
The licensee must have a mechanism of direct and frequent communication so that timely and accurate information on 
problems of animal health, behavior, and well-being is conveyed to the attending veterinarian.

Correct by 5/15/26 
Corrected on 5/14/26 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative. 
 
 n
